title: Bank Statement Analysis for Child Care and Daycare Businesses
meta: Daycare and childcare businesses have tuition-based recurring revenue. Learn how lenders analyze childcare bank statements for SBA loans, working capital, and business financing.
content:
Child Care Business Revenue Characteristics

Licensed daycare centers and home-based childcare providers are essential small businesses with distinctive bank statement patterns. Understanding these helps lenders correctly evaluate childcare operators applying for business loans, SBA financing, or facility expansion.

How Child Care Revenue Appears on Statements

  • Parent tuition payments: Regular monthly payments (first of month) from enrolled families — often ACH or check deposits
  • Childcare subsidy payments: Government subsidy programs (CCAP, Head Start partners, state voucher programs) pay via ACH, typically bi-weekly or monthly
  • Before/after school program fees: Additional revenue during school year
  • Registration and activity fees: Smaller periodic deposits at the start of enrollment periods

The combination of regular parent payments and government subsidy deposits creates a fairly predictable, recurring deposit pattern — one of the stronger income profiles in small business lending.

Government Subsidy Revenue

For centers that accept government childcare subsidies, this revenue source needs identification. Subsidies typically appear as state agency or county department ACH deposits and represent genuine, contractually assured income. AI tools like StatementScrub recognize these deposit patterns and classify them as income.

Operational Cost Structure

Childcare has high fixed costs:

  • Staff wages (largest cost — childcare requires mandated staff-to-child ratios)
  • Facility rent or mortgage
  • Food program costs
  • Supplies and materials
  • Licensing and insurance

Payroll outflows will be a large portion of withdrawals — expected and normal for this labor-intensive business.

Key Metrics for Childcare Lending

  • Average monthly deposits — should be consistent with enrollment capacity
  • Government subsidy payment regularity — confirms compliance with subsidy programs
  • Payroll consistency — confirms licensed staffing ratios are maintained
  • NSF history — a licensed daycare with NSF events has serious problems
  • Seasonal enrollment patterns — summer may show lower enrollment (school-age children leave)

SBA Lending for Childcare

The SBA considers childcare an eligible and desirable business type. SBA 7(a) and SBA 504 loans are commonly used for:

  • Facility acquisition or construction
  • Equipment (commercial kitchen, playground equipment, furniture)
  • Business acquisition when buying an existing center

Related: Bank statement analysis for SBA loans | Small business cash flow red flags

Bottom Line

Licensed childcare businesses with stable enrollment and government subsidy revenue are solid lending candidates. Their recurring tuition-based revenue model creates a deposit pattern that's predictable and verifiable through bank statement analysis.
